Your Final Major Project (FMP) is the final 10-week module of your Level 3 course. This project gives you the chance to plan and present your own small business idea based on the theme of sustainability. You will show that you can research useful information, use business and design ideas, and explain your plan clearly in a Creative Business Document using a Canva template.

By completing this project, you will develop important skills such as enhanced familiarity with academic, technological, and practical skills, planning your time, meeting deadlines, and communicating professionally. These are skills that are needed in real jobs like marketing, hotel management, running your own business, or coordinating a fashion brand. These skills will also help you succeed in future university-level study.
Overall, you should be able to demonstrate the following learning outcomes upon successful completion of the module:

You are asked to complete a Creative Business Document using a template selected from Canva. Please read the Creative Business Document guideline (available on Canvas) alongside this Assessment Brief.

You are required to use Canva to develop and format your project. Canva is the preferred platform because it offers a wide range of features that support professional design, layout, and presentation. You may use Microsoft PowerPoint only as a formatting tool. However, your final submission must NOT be in PowerPoint slide format. PowerPoint slides should be used solely to help you structure your content before exporting it into PDF which is an appropriate/required format for this assignment.

This document will present your sustainable business idea, related to your chosen pathway of either Business, Hospitality, or Fashion Management. Your idea should solve a real-world problem related to sustainability and show that you can plan, design, and communicate a creative business concept in a clear and professional way.
You must develop your project week by week with the help of lecturer and support of academic mentor to ensure that you gradually learn the required skills.

Important: To pass this assessment, you must have your laptop/tablet for ALL sessions in class as it requires various programs. Please note that it cannot be completed on a mobile phone and your class will not be timetabled in a computer lab. Sharing a laptop is not recommended as each student needs his/her own device for independent work.

The table(s) below show every task that you must complete within the Creative Business Document. You can use it like a checklist to complete one step at a time. The sequence below also follows the same order as the Creative Business Document Guideline available on Canvas. You will also see that this guide gives you suggested word counts for more detailed sections.

Please complete the ‘LCCA Student Ethical Approval Form’ by the end of Week 2 (maximum) before undertaking any primary research activity (e.g. surveys, interviews, data collection). This ensures your work can be reviewed and approved by the relevant academics in a timely manner.

Where a student undertakes primary research without approval, this may be considered a breach of academic and professional conduct and may be addressed in line with college policies. It may also impact the marks awarded where ethical requirements have not been met.

Assessment and feedback are aligned to UCA’s Generic Grading Criteria for level 3 study. Formative assessment submission (draft submission/work in progress) is due in Week 5. You are required to select a large UK-based organisation that operates in a competitive industry and has a well-established Learning and Development (L&D) team. This can be a public or private organisation. Suitable examples include UK Power Networks; Roadchef; People's Partnership; Blacklock, BT Group (British Telecom), British Airways, John Lewis & Partners. The following tasks needs to be completed and creativity is highly encouraged.
Prepare a comprehensive profile of your selected organisation. Go beyond basic facts and   provide analytical detail on the following areas:

The organisation’s mission and vision statements, together with its core values.
Conclude this section with a focused description of the organisation’s Learning & Development function — its role within the business, and how it supports the organisation’s strategic objectives.
Investigate the firm’s approach to learning and talent development. Your analysis should include:
How employees are trained and developed (delivery methods, platforms, on-the-job learning, apprenticeships, e-learning, partnerships, etc.).

Principal programmes offered (for example: reskilling/upskilling, leadership development, graduate schemes, technical training) and the specific aims of those programmes (career progression, succession planning, capability building, retention).
Choose two established Learning & Development theories or models and critically evaluate them in the context of your chosen organisation. For each model:
Critically assess the model’s effectiveness in relation to employee wellbeing — consider effects on job satisfaction, motivation, productivity, psychological safety or stress reduction.

Declaration: At the end of the assessment, you should also include a declaration of any software tools including Generative AI (GAI) applications that you used in developing and completing the assessment.

Excellent (70%+): Students will provide an excellent synthesis of the topic, demonstrating critical insight and excellent understanding of complex matters.

Focused and justified recommendations will be made as to how problems/weaknesses identified in the topic may be overcome, and sustainability improved. Extensive research demonstrating use of a wide range of well selected secondary research sources will be evident. Academic writing style, English and referencing will be excellent.

Very Good (60–69%): Students will provide a perceptive synthesis of the topic, demonstrating critical insight and clear understanding of complex matters. Well thought through and justified recommendations will be made as to how problems/weaknesses identified in the topic may be overcome, and sustainability improved. Research demonstrating use of a wide range of current and well selected secondary research sources will be evident. Academic writing style, English and referencing will be very good.

Good (50-59%): Students will provide a sound synthesis of the topic, demonstrating a clear and critical understanding of complex matters. Justified recommendations will be made as to how problems/weaknesses identified in the topic may be overcome, and sustainability improved. Research demonstrating use of a range of current and relevant secondary research sources will be evident. Academic writing style, English and referencing will be good.

Satisfactory (40-49%): Students will provide a satisfactory synthesis of the topic, demonstrating sufficient understanding of complex matters. Considered recommendations will be made as to how problems/weaknesses identified in the topic may be overcome, and sustainability improved. Research demonstrating use of a range of current secondary research sources will be evident. Academic writing style, English and referencing will be generally competent.
Fail (39% and below): Students who do not meet the requirements of a third-class grade will not successfully complete the assessment activity.

ASSESSMENT BRIEF

Declaration: At the end of the assessment, you should also include a declaration of any software tools including Generative AI (GAI) applications that you used in developing and completing the assessment.

Write a dissertation of 8000 words (±10%). Your dissertation is a structured report on the process and outcomes of a significant piece of independent research.  In this you will examine the context of your research to provide motivation for your work; you will clarify the aims and objectives of your proposed research; demonstrate critical engagement with key areas of relevant literature, using this to develop an initial conceptual framework; you will present and justify the theoretical perspective underpinning your proposed research approach, contrasting this with alternative perspectives, and use this to inform the systematic development of your research design;  you will present and discuss your findings and their implications, and consider the limitations of your proposed approach; you will also reflect on your contributions and to what extent your proposed objectives were met.

Dissertations follow a standard format; a proforma with guidelines, structure and formatting requirements will be available on the Canvas. An indication of the main structure and contents is given below.
Level HE6 (Dissertation) - It is expected that the Reference List will contain between thirty to fifty sources, with no fewer than twenty high quality academic journal articles.
See section 12 of the module guide for Guidelines on the Preparation and Submission of Assignments.

You are required to submit a soft copy via “Turn-it-in UK” on the module Canvas page. The software will allow you to check your work against other material on the internet. It will also compare your work against other students work.
You will be able to upload drafts of your written work into turn-it-in as many times as you wish before the deadline.
You will not be able to overwrite the final submission after the deadline. You can only submit a single file.
Dissertation Presentation Guidelines
The following guidelines should be used in preparing your written document in order that all dissertations conform to a similar style and structure.
The dissertation should be 8,000 words in length (excluding cover page, contents page, tables, figures, reference list and appendices).

Word Count. You are expected to revise and edit your dissertation to remain within +/- 10% of the allocated word limit for that piece of work. In order to ensure that word counts can easily be checked you should include:

A note of the word count, as performed by your word processing package, should be included in the dissertation. A deduction is made for all tables, figures, appendices and bibliography, which DO NOT count towards the overall word limit.
It is likely to be an exceptional piece of work that covers the dissertation requirements fully in much less than the set word countless 10%.

Abstract

This is a summary of about 250-500 words which describes the issue under investigation and the aims of the study. It should also briefly identify the main conclusions and recommendations. It should be no longer than one page.

Contents Page
This should show the reader how the report is structured and how the author has developed their approach to the problem. The contents page should be presented in the following manner:

It is likely throughout the dissertation that you will want to use tables or diagrams to present information. If these are directly related to your arguments it is better to include them in the main body, rather than the appendices. In such cases, it is conventional to provide a special "contents page" – List of Figures/List of Tables – which identify clearly where tables and figures are located.
Moreover, when you have more than one Appendix, you should include a special "contents page" listing all the Appendices.

The introduction should set the context and tone for the rest of the dissertation. You should outline the reasons giving rise to the problem or issue to be identified. It should give a broad introduction to the topic under review and the issues raised. The main point is to set clear objective(s) that you are seeking to achieve by the end of the dissertation report, which should be relatively concise and precise.

The review should examine the relevant literature and develop a conceptual and theoretical framework, based on models and theories, which will be used to analyse the topic under investigation. It should also involve a synthesis and a critical evaluation of current knowledge and background material on the topic being explored. Information from textbooks, academic and industry journals, newspapers and organisational documentation should be discussed, compared and contrasted in a structured and critical manner. Write selectively and focus on what is directly related to your topic. The review may consist of a number of sections or subsections.

This section should provide a critical discussion of the research methods and approaches considered and your rationale for the research design developed. The issue of triangulation and validity in the research design needs to be covered in detail together with a critical discussion and evaluation of the design framework. Any limitations in the research design that emerged from the investigation should be acknowledged. The methodology should provide sufficient detail to allow your supervisor to assess the reliability and validity of your methods.

In this section the results of your primary research should be presented and analysed, in a clear and concise manner. You will need to determine the key elements of your study and structure your results accordingly (it is not appropriate to simply list responses to questionnaires/interviews in question order). Your analysis and evaluation of data should be clearly linked to and supported by the academic concepts discussed in your literature review. This section may include tables (numerical information), figures (qualitative responses, graphs, charts) and photographs to illustrate points.

Your conclusions should naturally flow from your discussion and analysis of your findings. The conclusions should include a summary of your main findings which can be brought to bear on the defined objectives of the study. You should also include a discussion about the alternative courses of action available and their feasibility, desirability and acceptability to the organisation. Your conclusions need to be referenced back to your Literature Review and Findings and Analysis. The recommendations should emerge from the conclusions and should be feasible and practical: where costs are likely to be incurred, there needs to be a justification of the likely benefits. In addition, you need to consider and identify the possible human, political and managerial implications of any recommendations. This section should not introduce new material.

You will be allocated a Dissertation Supervisor at the beginning of semester 2. The Supervisor will be responsible for guiding you through the dissertation module. To maximise your chances of gaining a good grade, it is important that you make an appointment with your supervisor as soon as is practical. It is the student’s responsibility to contact his or her supervisor.

Your dissertation supervisor is an important resource who can give you advice on how to organise, conduct and produce your dissertation. The responsibilities of the dissertation supervisor are as follows:
Your dissertation supervisor must approve your dissertation title.  Any change in the title and/or subject matter of your dissertation must be agreed in writing by your supervisor.
ideas and plans. Your supervisor can indicate lines of thought that you may not have considered, or ask questions which will force you to explain and justify your ideas and
Your dissertation supervisor will be the first assessor of your dissertation. A sample of all of the students’ dissertations is internally and externally moderated.

Although the dissertation has been built around the concept of independent learning this does not mean that the student will be given total responsibility for controlling the timeline for the production of the dissertation. As part of the assessed workshops programme you will agree a series of meetings designed to monitor and support your progress.

You must consult your supervisor at regular agreed intervals. You must make an appointment with your supervisor to discuss each piece of draft work that you submit. The appointment should be made at the time that you submit each draft.

Drafts of the dissertation should be submitted to the dissertation supervisor, subject to prior arrangement. Failure to submit draft work at the stated times is likely to have a significant impact on your final grade for the dissertation.

Failure to meet with your supervisor could lead to failure of the module. It is your responsibility to contact your dissertation supervisor to arrange meetings to discuss your progress. The importance of these key stage progress meetings cannot be over-emphasised and are designed to act as deadlines to motivate you to achieve particular stages of the dissertation and obtain feedback from your supervisor.

Formal summative feedback is provided on the standard feedback form below after your submission. Because of its length and complexity dissertation coursework in this module is not usually marked on Grade mark. However, during your supervision you can expect to receive significant and frequent formative feedback.

Excellent (70%+): Your dissertation will be of excellent quality, where you exhibit coherent and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity.

Very Good (60–69%): Your dissertation will be of very good quality, where you exhibit coherent and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity.

Good (50–59%): Your dissertation will be of good quality, where you exhibit coherent and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity.

Satisfactory (40-49%):  Your dissertation will demonstrate a satisfactory display of coherent and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity.

Unsatisfactory (1-39%): Your dissertation will demonstrate a limited coherence and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity. Your proposal will demonstrate a lack of basic coherence and lucidity in the way in which it is written to demonstrate that it will be fit for purpose and able to direct your research activity.

ASSESSMENT BRIEF
LO2: Produce a critical literature review and research methodology relevant to chosen topic.

Declaration: At the end of the assessment, you should also include a declaration of any software tools including Generative AI (GAI) applications that you used in developing and completing the assessment.

Write a dissertation of 8000 words (±10%). Your dissertation is a structured report on the process and outcomes of a significant piece of independent research.  In this you will examine the context of your research to provide motivation for your work; you will clarify the aims and objectives of your proposed research; demonstrate critical engagement with key areas of relevant literature, using this to develop an initial conceptual framework; you will present and justify the theoretical perspective underpinning your proposed research approach, contrasting this with alternative perspectives, and use this to inform the systematic development of your research design;  you will present and discuss your findings and their implications, and consider the limitations of your proposed approach; you will also reflect on your contributions and to what extent your proposed objectives were met.

Dissertations follow a standard format; a proforma with guidelines, structure and formatting requirements will be available on the Canvas. An indication of the main structure and contents is given below.
Level HE6 (Dissertation) - It is expected that the Reference List will contain between thirty to fifty sources, with no fewer than twenty high quality academic journal articles.
See section 12 of the module guide for Guidelines on the Preparation and Submission of Assignments.

You are required to submit a soft copy via “Turn-it-in UK” on the module Canvas page. The software will allow you to check your work against other material on the internet. It will also compare your work against other students work.
You will be able to upload drafts of your written work into turn-it-in as many times as you wish before the deadline.
You will not be able to overwrite the final submission after the deadline. You can only submit a single file.
Dissertation Presentation Guidelines
The following guidelines should be used in preparing your written document in order that all dissertations conform to a similar style and structure.
The dissertation should be 8,000 words in length (excluding cover page, contents page, tables, figures, reference list and appendices).

Word Count. You are expected to revise and edit your dissertation to remain within +/- 10% of the allocated word limit for that piece of work. In order to ensure that word counts can easily be checked you should include:

A note of the word count, as performed by your word processing package, should be included in the dissertation. A deduction is made for all tables, figures, appendices and bibliography, which DO NOT count towards the overall word limit.
It is likely to be an exceptional piece of work that covers the dissertation requirements fully in much less than the set word countless 10%.

This is a summary of about 250-500 words which describes the issue under investigation and the aims of the study. It should also briefly identify the main conclusions and recommendations. It should be no longer than one page.

Contents Page
This should show the reader how the report is structured and how the author has developed their approach to the problem. The contents page should be presented in the following manner:

It is likely throughout the dissertation that you will want to use tables or diagrams to present information. If these are directly related to your arguments it is better to include them in the main body, rather than the appendices. In such cases, it is conventional to provide a special "contents page" – List of Figures/List of Tables – which identify clearly where tables and figures are located.
Moreover, when you have more than one Appendix, you should include a special "contents page" listing all the Appendices.

The introduction should set the context and tone for the rest of the dissertation. You should outline the reasons giving rise to the problem or issue to be identified. It should give a broad introduction to the topic under review and the issues raised. The main point is to set clear objective(s) that you are seeking to achieve by the end of the dissertation report, which should be relatively concise and precise.

The review should examine the relevant literature and develop a conceptual and theoretical framework, based on models and theories, which will be used to analyse the topic under investigation. It should also involve a synthesis and a critical evaluation of current knowledge and background material on the topic being explored. Information from textbooks, academic and industry journals, newspapers and organisational documentation should be discussed, compared and contrasted in a structured and critical manner. Write selectively and focus on what is directly related to your topic. The review may consist of a number of sections or subsections.

This section should provide a critical discussion of the research methods and approaches considered and your rationale for the research design developed. The issue of triangulation and validity in the research design needs to be covered in detail together with a critical discussion and evaluation of the design framework. Any limitations in the research design that emerged from the investigation should be acknowledged. The methodology should provide sufficient detail to allow your supervisor to assess the reliability and validity of your methods.

In this section the results of your primary research should be presented and analysed, in a clear and concise manner. You will need to determine the key elements of your study and structure your results accordingly (it is not appropriate to simply list responses to questionnaires/interviews in question order). Your analysis and evaluation of data should be clearly linked to and supported by the academic concepts discussed in your literature review. This section may include tables (numerical information), figures (qualitative responses, graphs, charts) and photographs to illustrate points.

Your conclusions should naturally flow from your discussion and analysis of your findings. The conclusions should include a summary of your main findings which can be brought to bear on the defined objectives of the study. You should also include a discussion about the alternative courses of action available and their feasibility, desirability and acceptability to the organisation. Your conclusions need to be referenced back to your Literature Review and Findings and Analysis. The recommendations should emerge from the conclusions and should be feasible and practical: where costs are likely to be incurred, there needs to be a justification of the likely benefits. In addition, you need to consider and identify the possible human, political and managerial implications of any recommendations. This section should not introduce new material.

It is the student’s responsibility to contact his or her supervisor.

Your dissertation supervisor is an important resource who can give you advice on how to organise, conduct and produce your dissertation. The responsibilities of the dissertation supervisor are as follows:
Your dissertation supervisor must approve your dissertation title.  Any change in the title and/or subject matter of your dissertation must be agreed in writing by your supervisor.
ideas and plans. Your supervisor can indicate lines of thought that you may not have considered, or ask questions which will force you to explain and justify your ideas and
Your dissertation supervisor will be the first assessor of your dissertation. A sample of all of the students’ dissertations is internally and externally moderated.

Although the dissertation has been built around the concept of independent learning this does not mean that the student will be given total responsibility for controlling the timeline for the production of the dissertation. As part of the assessed workshops programme you will agree a series of meetings designed to monitor and support your progress.

You must consult your supervisor at regular agreed intervals. You must make an appointment with your supervisor to discuss each piece of draft work that you submit. The appointment should be made at the time that you submit each draft.

Drafts of the dissertation should be submitted to the dissertation supervisor, subject to prior arrangement. Failure to submit draft work at the stated times is likely to have a significant impact on your final grade for the dissertation.

Failure to meet with your supervisor could lead to failure of the module. It is your responsibility to contact your dissertation supervisor to arrange meetings to discuss your progress. The importance of these key stage progress meetings cannot be over-emphasised and are designed to act as deadlines to motivate you to achieve particular stages of the dissertation and obtain feedback from your supervisor.

Assessment Details

All assignments should be submitted through Turnitin. If you file will not upload please ensure it is the correct size and if not you must contact the Admin Team (admin@lcca.org.uk) BEFORE the assignment Deadline.

Turnitin will review the assignment for levels of plagiarism and Academic Staff when marking will review this. Students who have been deemed as committing at academic offense including plagiarism or collusion will be subject to the Academic Misconduct policy.
Any work submitted after the deadline will be classed as a non-submission unless you have approved mitigating circumstances (see section).
All assignments will be given a mark out of 100. A pass mark will be 40 or above.
Students who fail their first attempt will be offered a resit attempt where the mark of the assignment will be capped at 40% at the Board of Examiners.
All Assignments at LCCA are marked in like with the Assessment Criteria outlined in Table A4, but also against these marking descriptors which outline the requirements for each grade banding.

LCCA and UCA understand that in student’s lives, there can be situations that are unexpected and can affect your ability to hand in your assignment. LCCA and UCA define these situations as mitigating circumstances, and these are exceptional, short-term events, which are outside the student’s control and affect their ability to prepare for, submit or present themselves for an assessment by the given deadline.

If something happens close to your assessment deadline that is hindering your ability to submit, please collect a Mitigating Circumstances from the Admin Team and submit along with appropriate supporting evidence BEFORE the assessment deadline. Any forms submitted after this deadline will require additional evidence.

However, LCCA and UCA consider that students who submit or present themselves for assessment are, in doing so, declaring themselves fit to be assessed, and therefore mitigating circumstances will not be accepted.
Academic Misconduct
Academic Misconduct is defined as where a student gains or seeks to gain an advantage in an assessment by unfair or improper means. Academic Misconduct can include the following:
Academic Misconduct
Academic Misconduct

Plagiarism, including self-plagiarism. This is where a student present work which contained unacknowledged published work (words, thoughts, judgements, ideas, structures, images etc.) of another, or presents work that has been previously submitted for another unit or course, or at a different institution.

Collusion. This is where a student work for assessment done in collaboration with another person(s) entirely as his or her own or collaborates with another student(s) on work, which is subsequently submitted as entirely of the other students work.
Collusion. This

Examination Offenses. This is where a student takes unauthorised materials into an exam room, communicates, or attempts to communicate with another student during the exam, fails to comply with invigilators instructions, or breaches other exam regulations.

Dishonest Practice. This can include using essay mills, submitting work not completed by yourself, offering bribes, seeking to obtain confidential information, making false declarations and falsifying transcripts and certification or other official documentation.
All assignments are checked for Academic Misconduct and students who have been found to commit an offense will be subject to the Academic Misconduct Policy and or the Student Disciplinary Procedures.

Artificial Intelligence (e.g. Chat GPT)

With reference to the use of AI Technologies, the UCA’s Academic Misconduct Policy defines plagiarism as - where student presents work for assessment which contains the unacknowledged published or unpublished words, thoughts, judgements, ideas, structures or images of another person or persons. This includes material downloaded from digital sources and material obtained from third parties including online essay mills and AI applications.
Academic Misconduct Policy

To enhance student learning, enable students to explore new ideas and concepts; undertake effective research; and help with assessment planning, the University supports the appropriate use of AI technologies, though students should give serious consideration as to how they may choose to use such technologies within assessments they submit.
If a student uses AI technologies for any part of their assessment - planning, research or content - they must:
explain how the AI content has been reused within the assessment. In such cases, the student will have acted with integrity and academic misconduct will not have been committed.
Although, it is important to acknowledge that grades are determined of by evidence of meeting learning objectives at a particular level of competency, not on repetition of AI generated content.

If the University suspects that AI technologies have been used during the assessment process and submission, then it reserves the right to invite the student to attend a viva (oral examination) to explore the student’s understanding of their submission. If evidence of academic misconduct is found, then the case will be addressed in accordance with the University’s Academic Misconduct Policy.
Academic Misconduct Policy
You should expect to receive you marks approximately 4 weeks after the assignment deadline.

To ensure fairness and parity of marking, all assignments are first marked, a sample are second marked, and Internally Verified by a tutor from UCA to ensure the marks are correct and the feedback is appropriate.
The marks also considered by an Internal Exam Board at LCCA to ensure due process has been followed and the Board of Examiners at UCA where marks are ratified.
Your provisional grade and feedback will be made available to you on Turnitin on Canvas, unless advised otherwise by your tutor.

1. Introduction

ArtBridge is a proposed community arts gallery designed to support disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives through accessible creative opportunities. The project combines social impact with financial sustainability by operating as a small-scale social enterprise with community-focused objectives.

The gallery will create a safe and inclusive creative environment where young people can participate in visual arts, music, poetry, performance and community events. At the same time, ArtBridge seeks to support local artists by offering opportunities for exhibitions, performances, mentoring and networking.
The gallery will initially operate as a small-scale pilot project to ensure that it remains realistic, manageable and financially sustainable within the scope of the module.
1.1 Background and Project Initiation

The group identified a need for more accessible creative opportunities for disadvantaged young people and local creatives within the community. Many young people experience social isolation, limited access to creative spaces and reduced opportunities for self-expression. At the same time, many emerging artists struggle to find affordable spaces to showcase their work and connect with the community.

ArtBridge was developed as a response to these issues by creating a community-focused arts gallery that promotes creativity, inclusion and social engagement. The project has been designed as a realistic and manageable pilot initiative that can begin on a small scale before any future expansion.
1.2 Innovation and Social Value

ArtBridge combines elements of a community arts initiative and a social enterprise model to create both social and creative value within the local community. The project is innovative because it focuses not only on artistic activities, but also on youth inclusion, community participation and accessible creative opportunities.

Unlike a traditional gallery, ArtBridge aims to create an interactive and inclusive environment where disadvantaged young people, local artists and community members can collaborate through workshops, exhibitions, performances and mentoring activities.
The project also promotes social sustainability by encouraging community engagement, supporting emerging creatives and reducing barriers to creative participation.
2. Project Aim and Objectives
2.1 Aim

To develop a small-scale, financially sustainable community arts gallery that supports disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives through accessible workshops, exhibitions, mentoring and community events.
2.2 Objectives
To deliver a programme of at least 12 inclusive creative workshops in visual art, music, poetry and performance during the 12-month pilot.

To create opportunities for emerging local artists by delivering at least one community exhibition or open mic event during the pilot, alongside ongoing exhibition, poetry and performance opportunities.
To develop a mixed-income funding model using grants, donations, memberships, ticketed events, venue hire, sponsorship and art sales commission.

To establish at least two or three active partnerships with local schools, youth organisations, charities or businesses to secure participant referrals and shared resources, with scope to involve universities and councils.
3. Scope and Deliverables
3.1 Scope Statement

This project will focus on developing a small-scale community arts gallery that supports disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives through workshops, exhibitions, mentoring and community events.

The project will include planning the gallery concept, identifying stakeholders and partnerships, developing a funding model, creating marketing ideas and designing community-focused creative activities.

The project will not include full operational delivery, clinical support services, formal education programmes, national expansion or large-scale commercial operations. It will also not provide counselling, housing support or a full café/bar operation at the initial stage. The wider scope set out in this section represents the project’s longer-term vision; for the initial 12-month pilot the team will concentrate on a smaller set of top priorities, defined in section 3.5 below.

3.4 Target Beneficiaries

The project will mainly benefit disadvantaged young people, emerging local creatives and community members who want access to creative opportunities. It will also support local artists, schools, youth organisations, charities and community partners.
3.5 Initial Stage Priorities

To keep the project realistic and manageable, the pilot will focus on a small number of top priorities during the first 12 months. Wider activities, stakeholders and income sources described elsewhere in this proposal will be considered for later development, once the pilot is established and the team has built the necessary partnerships, audience and operational experience.
Establishing a single community gallery space and a small programme of monthly creative workshops focused on disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives.
Building active partnerships with two or three local schools, youth organisations or charities to secure consistent participant referrals and shared resources.
Securing core pilot funding through one or two grant applications, combined with local sponsorship and community donations, to cover essential setup and delivery costs.
Launching a basic social media presence and simple promotional materials to build awareness, attract participants and engage the local community.
Delivering one small community exhibition or open mic event during the pilot to demonstrate impact, evaluate results and build a case for further development.

These priorities reflect what the team can realistically achieve within the 12-month pilot lifecycle with limited resources, and they form the basis of the stages set out in the Work Breakdown Structure, the Gantt chart timeline and the task allocation that follow.
4. Funding and Income Model

ArtBridge will operate using a mixed-income model to reduce financial risk and improve sustainability. Possible sources of income include grants, donations, sponsorships, membership schemes, ticketed events, venue hire, art sales commission and community partnerships.

5. Stakeholder Analysis and Communication
5.1 Stakeholder Identification

Stakeholders are individuals, groups or organisations that are affected by the project, can influence the project or have an interest in its success. For the ArtBridge project, stakeholders include people directly involved in the project, people who benefit from it and organisations that may support or influence its development.

5.3 Stakeholder Analysis

The stakeholder analysis shows that some stakeholders have higher levels of power and influence than others. Project team members, sponsors and local councils are important because they can influence funding, approvals and project direction.
Disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives are also key stakeholders because they are the main beneficiaries of the project and will directly experience its impact.
Community members, volunteers and local businesses have lower influence but remain important for long-term support, participation and sustainability.

5.5 Communication Plan

Effective communication is important because different stakeholders require different levels of information and engagement. Communication methods should match stakeholder needs, influence and involvement in the project.
5.6 Communication Strategy

The communication strategy for ArtBridge focuses on maintaining clear, realistic and consistent communication with stakeholders. High-power stakeholders such as sponsors and project team members require more regular updates because they directly influence project decisions and resources.
Stakeholders with high interest but lower power, such as young people and local creatives, must still receive regular information to encourage participation and engagement.

The project will mainly use affordable and accessible communication methods such as email, WhatsApp, meetings, posters and social media platforms. These methods are realistic for a small-scale community project and suitable for the available resources.

8. Risk Management

Risk management is important because projects may face challenges that could affect time, cost, quality or delivery. Identifying potential risks early allows the team to plan mitigation strategies and reduce the likelihood of disruption.
8.1 Risk Identification and Categories

As the project plan developed, the team carried out a more detailed risk assessment using a formal risk matrix. This structured assessment covers risk identification, scoring each risk for likelihood and impact, plotting the risks in a matrix and deciding on a prioritised response. Of the six risk categories commonly used in project management (time, resource/cost, communication, delivery, participation and teamwork), three were judged most critical to a small-scale community arts pilot: resource/cost, participation and delivery. Five representative risks were identified for each category, giving fifteen risks in total.
8.2 Scoring Likelihood and Impact

Each of the fifteen risks was scored on two dimensions: how likely it is to occur (likelihood) and how serious its effect on the project would be (impact). Each dimension uses three levels: Low, Medium and High. Likelihood and impact are shown in dedicated columns alongside each risk so the scores can be compared at a glance.
8.3 The Risk Matrix

The fifteen risks have been plotted in the three-by-three matrix below. Each number corresponds to the risk number in the scoring table above; where several risks share the same likelihood and impact score, their numbers appear together in the same cell. Cell colours follow standard risk-management convention: green for low risk, amber for medium risk and red for high risk.
8.4 Risk Response and Prioritisation

Based on its position in the matrix, each risk has been assessed for the action required. Red-zone risks require urgent action, amber-zone risks require a clear mitigation plan, and green-zone risks can be accepted with routine monitoring through the existing communication plan. The table below lists the risks in order of priority: the red-zone risk first, followed by all amber-zone risks in numerical order, and finally the green-zone risks in numerical order.

The risk matrix focuses on the three categories considered most critical to the success of an early-stage community arts pilot: resource/cost, participation and delivery. Of the fifteen risks identified, one sits in the high-priority red zone (low workshop and event turnout) and requires urgent proactive action from the start of the project. A further eleven risks sit in the medium amber zone and require clear mitigation plans, while three risks can be safely monitored through routine project oversight.
9. Project Analysis
9.1 Iron Triangle: Time, Cost and Quality

Time: The project is designed as a realistic small-scale pilot delivered across a fixed 12-month project lifecycle (January to December 2026), beginning with planning, partnerships and funding before launching workshops and events.
Cost and Resources: Costs will be controlled by starting small and using a mixed-income funding model supported by partnerships, volunteers and external funding opportunities.
Quality: The project must maintain safe, inclusive and well-organised creative activities that provide meaningful value to participants and the wider community.
Overall Judgement: The project is realistic and manageable if it remains focused on its core objectives and avoids scope creep.
9.2 Risk of Scope Creep
One of the main risks for the project is scope creep. This could happen if the project attempts to provide too many services too quickly or expand beyond the original aim.
To avoid this, the group will continue focusing on the original objectives, prioritise the main deliverables and keep the project realistic and manageable.
9.3 Task Allocation

Task allocation ensures that responsibilities are shared fairly across the group. Each member has a main area of responsibility while still contributing to overall project development and decision-making.

The table below shows how responsibilities have been distributed across the six members of the project team. Each role maps directly to one or more stages from the Work Breakdown Structure (section 6) and the corresponding timeline in the Gantt chart (section 7), so the three sections together describe the same plan from three perspectives: what will be done, when it will happen and who is responsible.
The group will continue working collaboratively through shared communication, regular updates and joint review meetings. This approach helps reduce confusion and improves accountability.
10. Success Criteria
12 creative workshops and one community exhibition or open mic event are delivered during the pilot;
11. Conclusion

ArtBridge is a realistic and socially valuable community project that combines creativity, inclusion and sustainability. It sits between a charity and a business: it has a social mission, but uses income-generating activities to remain financially sustainable.

The project provides opportunities for disadvantaged young people and emerging local creatives while also encouraging community engagement and cultural participation. By operating as a small-scale social enterprise with a mixed-income model, the project aims to balance social impact with financial sustainability.

The planning process - from clarifying the project aim and scope, through identifying stakeholders and developing a communication plan, to building a Work Breakdown Structure and Gantt Chart - has created a stronger, more manageable project structure. The Work Breakdown Structure breaks the project into manageable stages, and the Gantt Chart organises those stages into a realistic timeline. Together they support improved organisation, communication and task allocation.
ArtBridge is manageable if it remains focused, realistic and small-scale at the beginning, providing a strong foundation for future development and a meaningful contribution to the local community.
